items.q: What does it mean that RAM is volatile?
items.correct: Its contents are lost when power is removed.
items.misconception: It can explode or be damaged easily.
items.why: Students take 'volatile' from chemistry literally. In computing, volatile means contents don't persist without power - that's why RAM is used for working memory, not storage.

items.q: Which item is removed first from a stack?
items.why: Students confuse stacks (LIFO - like a stack of plates) with queues (FIFO - like a checkout line). The 'stack of plates' analogy makes LIFO concrete.

items.q: What is hexadecimal A in denary?
items.why: Students forget hex uses A-F to represent 10-15 because we need 16 distinct symbols. A=10, B=11, C=12, D=13, E=14, F=15.

items.q: Which idea breaks a problem into parts?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Decomposition. “Abstraction” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: Hiding unimportant details. “Adding more details” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many computational thinking ideas are there (decomposition, abstraction, pattern, algorithm)?
items.why: Exact answer: 4. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
